content.text: Controlling who can merge changes to prompts, tool schemas, and evaluation configuration is one of the most consequential governance decisions in an AI engineering team. The CODEOWNERS file makes that control explicit, automatic, and auditable — every pull request touching a sensitive path is blocked from merging until the designated owner approves it.

attrs.alt: CODEOWNERS enforcement flow: PR Opens → GitHub resolves owners from CODEOWNERS file and matches changed paths → Required reviewers auto-requested → Owners review and approve their paths → Merge allowed once all required owners have approved. Legend shows example path mappings for prompts, tools, evals, and model config.

content.text: What CODEOWNERS Does and How GitHub Enforces It
The
CODEOWNERS

file is a plain-text configuration file that maps file patterns to one or more GitHub users or teams. When a pull request is opened or updated, GitHub reads this file, determines which patterns match the changed files, and automatically requests review from the corresponding owners. If branch protection is configured to require code owner review, the PR cannot be merged until every required owner has approved.
The file lives in one of three locations in the repository: the repo root, inside
.github/
, or inside
docs/
. GitHub reads them in that order and uses the first one it finds. The pattern syntax closely follows
.gitignore
rules: a leading
anchors the pattern to the repo root, a trailing
matches directories recursively, and
matches any number of path segments.

GitHub enforcement has three layers. First, review requests are automatically sent to owners when a PR touches matching paths — owners do not need to be watching the repository. Second, branch protection rules on the target branch can set "Require review from Code Owners," which blocks the merge button until every owner whose pattern was matched has submitted an approving review. Third, when new commits are pushed to an open PR, any previously granted approvals from code owners are automatically dismissed, forcing a fresh review of the updated diff.
Integration with Branch Protection
The CODEOWNERS file is inert unless branch protection is enabled. Navigate to
Settings → Branches → Branch protection rules

and check "Require a pull request before merging," then "Require review from Code Owners." You can also set a minimum number of required approvals here — this count stacks with the code owner requirement rather than replacing it. A PR to
main
might require two approvals in total, but at least one must come from the specific code owner matched by the changed path.
Why AI Repos Need Strict CODEOWNERS

In a conventional software repository, a merge that slips past review will typically cause a bug that manifests at runtime, gets caught by monitoring, and is rolled back. The blast radius is bounded: one feature, one API, one service. In an AI repo, the blast radius is different in kind. A single-line change to a system prompt can subtly alter the model's persona, safety posture, or factual grounding across every request the system handles — often in ways that pass automated tests but are invisible until a user encounters the degraded behaviour.
Three asset categories carry disproportionate risk and must have explicit owners:
Prompts.

The system prompt and any message templates directly shape model behaviour. A change that adds a single instruction — "always end responses with a call to action" or "do not discuss competitor products" — takes effect immediately on the next request. No redeployment, no binary change, no staged rollout by default.
Tool definitions.

Tools execute real side effects: writing to databases, calling external APIs, spawning subprocesses. An incorrectly defined tool schema can cause the model to invoke a tool with unexpected arguments, or prevent a legitimate tool call from being routed correctly.
Eval and safety configuration.

Evaluation pass thresholds and red-team suite definitions are the gates that determine whether a model version ships. Loosening a threshold or removing a red-team case is equivalent to disabling a test — without the visibility of a deleted test file in a diff review.

CODEOWNERS makes human review of these assets mandatory at the version-control layer, which is the earliest and cheapest point to catch problems. The alternative — relying on post-merge monitoring, staged rollouts, or good intentions — discovers problems after users have already been affected.
Owning the Prompt Registry
A prompt registry centralises all system prompts, message templates, and few-shot examples in a single authoritative location. Common layouts store prompts as versioned JSON or YAML files under a
prompts/
directory, or as a single registry file such as
config/prompt-registry.json
. Every entry in the registry is a production artefact: changing it changes model output immediately on the next request that loads the prompt.
Prompt ownership should involve two distinct groups. An
AI safety team
or safety-focused individual reviews every change for potential safety, bias, or policy violations — does the new prompt create any path to harmful output? A
prompt authors team

comprising the product and ML engineers who write prompts day-to-day reviews for quality: does the instruction make sense, is it internally consistent with the rest of the prompt, will it degrade task performance?
Cover the registry file directly:
config/prompt-registry.json
maps to both the safety team and the prompt authors team.
Cover the prompt directory recursively:
prompts/**
catches new subdirectories and file types automatically.
Cover specific high-risk prompt files individually:
A
prompts/system.md
rule that adds the CTO or head of AI as an additional owner ensures that changes to the root system prompt have the highest level of approval.
Require two reviewers for the system prompt:
The root system prompt is the single highest-influence artefact in the repo; list two individuals or teams as owners so both must approve any change.
Owning Tool Definitions

Tool definitions describe what the model can call, what arguments it should pass, and (in implementation files) what code executes when a tool is invoked. These are split between schema files — JSON Schema objects that appear in the API request — and implementation files that contain the actual execution logic.

Both layers need ownership. A change to the schema that adds a new required parameter will cause the model to attempt to fill that parameter and may produce malformed calls if the prompt does not describe the new field. A change to the implementation that drops input validation, fails to sanitise a path argument, or removes a rate-limit check can cause data loss or security incidents regardless of what the model sends.
tools/*.json or tools/schemas/**
— schema files. Own these with the tool engineering team plus the ML engineer responsible for the affected agent.
tools/implementations/** or src/tools/**
— implementation files. Own these with the backend or platform engineering team in addition to tool engineers, since implementation changes carry execution risk.
Differentiate read-only from write tools.

A tool that reads from a database can be owned by a standard team. A tool that writes, deletes, or calls an external payment or identity API should have a senior engineer or security engineer as an additional owner.
Keep schema and implementation in the same PR when both change.
CODEOWNERS ensures both owner groups are requested; this is the right structure rather than a limitation to work around.
Owning Eval Configuration

Evaluation suites determine when a model version is safe to ship. The configuration that governs them — which test cases run, which judge model evaluates them, and what pass rate is required — is as important as the test cases themselves. A PR that raises a threshold from 0.80 to 0.85 increases quality requirements; a PR that lowers it from 0.80 to 0.70 weakens the gate. Both deserve careful review.
evals/
directory: own with eval leads, who understand what the suite covers and can judge whether a new test case is well-formed.
evals/config.yaml or evals/thresholds.json
: threshold files warrant a higher bar — require both an eval lead and an AI safety reviewer, since lowering a threshold is a quality regression.
evals/judges/
: judge configurations (which model evaluates outputs, what rubric it uses) affect the validity of every eval score. Own these with the eval leads plus the person responsible for eval infrastructure.
evals/datasets/
: test case datasets. Own these with the data team responsible for data quality and any domain expert for the subject matter of the eval.
Owning Safety Red-Team Suites

Red-team evaluation suites are distinct from functional evals and must be owned separately. Their purpose is adversarial: they test whether the model can be manipulated into producing harmful, biased, or policy-violating output. The people best placed to review changes to these suites are those focused on safety rather than product quality — a different group from the feature eval owners.

Separating red-team ownership also prevents conflicts of interest. A product engineer under deadline pressure who co-owns both the feature evals and the red-team suite faces an incentive to weaken the red-team tests to ship faster. Keeping red-team ownership exclusively with a safety team removes that structural pressure.
evals/red-team/**
: own exclusively with the AI safety team, not with feature engineers.
Require two approvals for any deletion of a red-team test case.
A deleted test case removes coverage silently. Two reviewers independently examining the justification reduces the risk of accidental or coerced coverage reduction.
Consider a separate CODEOWNERS file inside the evals/red-team/ directory.
GitHub supports per-directory CODEOWNERS overrides, enabling a stricter owner set for this subdirectory without affecting broader evals/ ownership.
Owning CI/CD Configuration

Pipeline configuration files control how tests run, which secrets are available, and what deployment steps execute. An unreviewed change to a GitHub Actions workflow could disable a safety eval step, inject a malicious deployment script, or grant a workflow permissions it should not have. CODEOWNERS ownership of
.github/workflows/
ensures that pipeline changes require review from a platform or DevOps engineer who understands GitHub Actions security.
.github/workflows/**
: own with the platform engineering team. They understand GitHub Actions permissions, secret scoping, and OIDC token usage.
.github/CODEOWNERS itself

: own this file with a senior engineering lead or CTO. A change to CODEOWNERS that removes an owner from a sensitive path is a governance change, not a code change, and deserves the same scrutiny as the assets it governs.
Makefile, Dockerfile, docker-compose.yaml
: if these drive CI steps or build AI-specific assets, include them in platform team ownership.
Owning Model Configuration
Model configuration files specify which model version is called, with what temperature, top-p, and other sampling parameters, and for which agent roles. Changing the model from
claude-3-5-haiku-20241022
to
claude-opus-4-5

changes cost by an order of magnitude and may change output quality and latency in ways that affect downstream systems. Changing temperature from 0.3 to 1.0 can make outputs dramatically less predictable across a production fleet.
config/model*.yaml
: own with the model operations team and the head of AI or CTO. Cost and quality tradeoffs at the model-selection level are leadership decisions.
config/agents/**
: per-agent configuration (which model each agent uses, its max tokens, its retry policy). Own with the model ops team plus the engineer responsible for that agent.
config/sampling.yaml or similar parameter files
: temperature, top-p, and seed values. Own with the ML team who can assess the impact of parameter changes on output distribution.
Full CODEOWNERS File for a GenAI Monorepo
The following is a complete
CODEOWNERS
file for a generative AI monorepo. It covers all the asset categories discussed above. Place it at
.github/CODEOWNERS

# .github/CODEOWNERS
# =============================================================================
# CODEOWNERS for a GenAI monorepo
# Format: <pattern>  <owner1>  [owner2 ...]
# Rules are evaluated top-to-bottom; the LAST matching rule wins.
# =============================================================================

# ----- Default fallback: all files require platform team review -----
*                              @myorg/platform-eng

# ----- Prompt Registry -----
# The root system prompt is the highest-influence artefact in the repo.
# Requires both the AI safety team and the head of AI.
prompts/system.md              @myorg/ai-safety  @cto-handle
prompts/system.yaml            @myorg/ai-safety  @cto-handle

# All other prompt files require ai-safety + prompt authors.
prompts/**                     @myorg/ai-safety  @myorg/prompt-authors
config/prompt-registry.json    @myorg/ai-safety  @myorg/prompt-authors

# ----- Tool Definitions -----
# Schemas: tool engineers + the ML lead for the relevant agent.
tools/schemas/**               @myorg/tool-engineers  @myorg/ml-leads
tools/definitions.json         @myorg/tool-engineers  @myorg/ml-leads

# Implementations: tool engineers + backend security review.
tools/implementations/**       @myorg/tool-engineers  @myorg/backend-security
src/tools/**                   @myorg/tool-engineers  @myorg/backend-security

# ----- Eval Configuration -----
# Red-team suites: safety team only (no feature engineers).
evals/red-team/**              @myorg/ai-safety

# Judge configuration: eval leads + eval-infra owner.
evals/judges/**                @myorg/eval-leads  @eval-infra-owner

# Threshold configuration: eval leads + ai-safety.
evals/thresholds.json          @myorg/eval-leads  @myorg/ai-safety
evals/config.yaml              @myorg/eval-leads  @myorg/ai-safety

# All other eval files: eval leads.
evals/**                       @myorg/eval-leads

# ----- Model Configuration -----
config/model*.yaml             @myorg/model-ops  @cto-handle
config/agents/**               @myorg/model-ops  @myorg/ml-leads
config/sampling.yaml           @myorg/model-ops  @myorg/ml-team

# ----- CI/CD Configuration -----
.github/workflows/**           @myorg/platform-eng
.github/CODEOWNERS             @myorg/platform-eng  @cto-handle
Makefile                       @myorg/platform-eng
Dockerfile                     @myorg/platform-eng
docker-compose*.yaml           @myorg/platform-eng

# ----- Infrastructure & secrets -----
infra/**                       @myorg/platform-eng  @myorg/security
terraform/**                   @myorg/platform-eng  @myorg/security
.env.example                   @myorg/platform-eng

# ----- Dependencies -----
pyproject.toml                 @myorg/platform-eng
requirements*.txt              @myorg/platform-eng
package.json                   @myorg/platform-eng
package-lock.json              @myorg/platform-eng
Pattern Precedence Rules in CODEOWNERS
GitHub evaluates CODEOWNERS rules in file order from top to bottom. The
last
matching rule wins — not the most specific one. This is the opposite of many routing systems and catches engineers by surprise. A
wildcard at the top of the file sets a default owner for everything. Any more-specific rule below it overrides the default for files that match both patterns.
If you write the rules in the wrong order, the specific rule can be overridden by the general one that comes after it. Consider this broken example:

# BROKEN: specific rule appears before general rule
prompts/system.md    @myorg/ai-safety  @cto-handle

# This rule comes later, so it overrides the specific rule above.
# system.md will only require @myorg/prompt-authors — cto-handle is lost.
prompts/**           @myorg/prompt-authors
The fix is to put the more general rule first, and the more specific rule last, so the specific rule wins:

# CORRECT: general rule first, specific rule last
prompts/**           @myorg/ai-safety  @myorg/prompt-authors

# This rule is later, so it wins for system.md specifically.
# system.md requires ai-safety + cto-handle (overrides the rule above).
prompts/system.md    @myorg/ai-safety  @cto-handle
Additional pattern rules to remember:
A pattern with a leading
anchors to the repo root. Without it, the pattern can match at any depth.
*.yaml
matches every YAML file in the entire repo;
/*.yaml
matches only YAML files in the root directory.
A trailing
matches a directory and all files within it recursively.
evals/
is equivalent to
evals/**
A pattern with no
is a glob matched against the filename only, not the path.
*.json
matches every JSON file anywhere in the repo.
Negation with
is not supported in CODEOWNERS (unlike
.gitignore
). The only way to exclude a file from an owner is to list it with a different (or empty) owner in a later, more-specific rule.
Team vs Individual Ownership
GitHub CODEOWNERS accepts both
@username
(individual) and
@org/team-name
(team) owners. The choice has practical consequences for review velocity and bus-factor.

When a team is listed as owner, GitHub requests a review from the team as a whole. Any member of the team can approve the PR on behalf of the team. This is resilient to individual absence — if one engineer is on vacation, another team member can approve. It also scales: as the team grows, new members automatically gain review authority without updating CODEOWNERS.

When an individual is listed, only that specific user's approval satisfies the requirement. This creates higher accountability — the named person is explicitly responsible — but also creates a bottleneck. If that person is unavailable, PRs queue up and team velocity drops. Individual ownership is appropriate for a small number of ultra-high-stakes paths (the root system prompt, the CODEOWNERS file itself, model version upgrades) where the bottleneck is a feature, not a bug.
Use team ownership
for all routine AI asset paths:
prompts/**
tools/**
evals/**
. Teams provide coverage without creating bottlenecks.
Use individual ownership

as an additional owner (not the sole owner) for the highest-risk paths: the root system prompt, the CODEOWNERS file itself, model name upgrades. List the team as the primary owner and the individual as an extra required approver.
Avoid solo individual ownership on busy paths.

If a single named individual is the only owner of a frequently-changed path, that person becomes a mandatory bottleneck on every PR touching that path. Even for senior engineers, this is unsustainable at scale.
Keep team membership updated.
A team in CODEOWNERS is only as good as its membership list. When an engineer leaves or changes roles, remove them from the relevant team. GitHub does not automatically prune departed members.
Use GitHub team discussions or README to document why a team owns what it owns.
CODEOWNERS records the assignment but not the rationale; documentation prevents future owners from unknowingly weakening coverage.
Validating CODEOWNERS with a CI Check
CODEOWNERS files are easy to get wrong silently. A typo in a team name (
@myorg/ai-safty
instead of
@myorg/ai-safety
) causes GitHub to ignore that owner entirely, so the path is effectively unowned. A CI step that validates the file on every PR prevents these errors from reaching the main branch.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# scripts/validate-codeowners.sh
# Validates that all sensitive AI asset paths have at least one explicit owner.
# Run as a required CI check on every PR.

set -euo pipefail

CODEOWNERS_FILE=".github/CODEOWNERS"

if [[ ! -f "$CODEOWNERS_FILE" ]]; then
  echo "ERROR: $CODEOWNERS_FILE not found" >&2
  exit 1
fi

# Define sensitive paths that MUST have an explicit owner entry.
# This list is the single source of truth for mandatory coverage.
REQUIRED_PATHS=(
  "prompts/"
  "prompts/system.md"
  "tools/schemas/"
  "tools/implementations/"
  "evals/red-team/"
  "evals/thresholds.json"
  "config/model"
  "config/agents/"
  ".github/workflows/"
  ".github/CODEOWNERS"
)

MISSING=()

for path in "${REQUIRED_PATHS[@]}"; do
  # Grep for any CODEOWNERS rule that starts with the path (or a containing glob).
  # This is a heuristic: a full parser would be more accurate but is overkill for
  # most repos. The check ensures at least one line mentions the path prefix.
  if ! grep -qE "^${path}" "$CODEOWNERS_FILE"; then
    MISSING+=("$path")
  fi
done

if [[ ${#MISSING[@]} -gt 0 ]]; then
  echo "ERROR: The following required paths have no CODEOWNERS entry:" >&2
  for p in "${MISSING[@]}"; do
    echo "  - $p" >&2
  done
  echo "" >&2
  echo "Add entries for these paths to $CODEOWNERS_FILE and re-run." >&2
  exit 1
fi

echo "CODEOWNERS validation passed: all required paths have coverage."

# Optionally: call the GitHub API to verify that listed teams/users exist.
# Requires GITHUB_TOKEN in the environment.
if [[ -n "${GITHUB_TOKEN:-}" ]]; then
  echo "Verifying team existence via GitHub API..."
  # Extract all @org/team-name tokens from the file.
  teams=$(grep -oE '@[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+/[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+' "$CODEOWNERS_FILE" | sort -u)
  for team in $teams; do
    org=$(echo "$team" | cut -d'/' -f1 | tr -d '@')
    slug=$(echo "$team" | cut -d'/' -f2)
    status=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" \
      -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
      "https://api.github.com/orgs/${org}/teams/${slug}")
    if [[ "$status" != "200" ]]; then
      echo "WARNING: Team $team returned HTTP $status — may not exist" >&2
    else
      echo "  OK: $team exists"
    fi
  done
fi
Wire this into a GitHub Actions workflow as a required check on all PRs targeting
main

# .github/workflows/validate-codeowners.yaml
name: Validate CODEOWNERS

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - '.github/CODEOWNERS'

jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Validate CODEOWNERS coverage
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
        run: bash scripts/validate-codeowners.sh
Triggering the check only when
.github/CODEOWNERS

changes keeps CI fast: the validation runs exactly when needed and not on every unrelated PR. If the required-paths list itself can change (for example when a new prompt subdirectory is added), also trigger on the paths that would create new sensitive locations.
Minimum Reviewers: 1 vs 2 for Prompt Changes
GitHub branch protection lets you set a minimum number of required approvals separately from code owner requirements. These settings compose: a PR that touches
prompts/system.md
might require the code owner approval (from
@myorg/ai-safety
and
@cto-handle
) plus a minimum of two approvals total. Because two distinct individuals are listed as owners, satisfying the code owner requirement automatically satisfies the minimum-review-count requirement.
A risk-based framework for setting the reviewer threshold:
One reviewer is sufficient

for low-risk prompt paths: adding a new few-shot example to a retrieval-augmented search prompt, adding a new tool description to the registry, updating a prompt that serves a single low-traffic internal tool.
Two reviewers are warranted

for any change to the system prompt, any change that adds or modifies a safety instruction, any change to a prompt that serves more than a small number of users, and any change that the PR author identifies as behaviorally significant.
Two reviewers from different teams are warranted
for the root system prompt of a consumer-facing product. One reviewer from the AI safety team and one from the product leadership team ensures both technical safety and business alignment sign-off.
Eval threshold changes always require two reviewers,
regardless of how small the change appears. Lowering a threshold by 2 percentage points is easy to overlook in a diff; a second set of eyes comparing old and new numbers catches the regression.
Do not require more than two reviewers for routine changes.

Three or more required approvals create friction that causes engineers to work around review requirements by making changes appear smaller or splitting them across PRs. Two reviewers from the right teams is the practical ceiling for most organisations.
GitHub CLI Script to Audit Current Owners
CODEOWNERS coverage drifts over time as new directories are added and ownership assignments change. The following script uses the GitHub CLI (
gh
) to query which users or teams own each of the sensitive paths, making it straightforward to audit coverage and detect gaps.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# scripts/audit-codeowners.sh
# Prints the resolved owners for a set of sensitive paths using the GitHub API.
# Requires: gh CLI authenticated (gh auth login), REPO in the format owner/repo.
#
# Usage: REPO=myorg/my-ai-repo bash scripts/audit-codeowners.sh

set -euo pipefail

REPO="${REPO:-}"
if [[ -z "$REPO" ]]; then
  echo "Usage: REPO=owner/repo bash $0" >&2
  exit 1
fi

# Paths to audit — extend this list to match your repo structure.
AUDIT_PATHS=(
  "prompts/system.md"
  "prompts/"
  "tools/schemas/tool.json"
  "tools/implementations/search_tool.py"
  "evals/red-team/"
  "evals/thresholds.json"
  "config/model-prod.yaml"
  "config/agents/"
  ".github/workflows/deploy.yaml"
  ".github/CODEOWNERS"
)

echo "Auditing CODEOWNERS for repo: $REPO"
echo "====================================================="

for path in "${AUDIT_PATHS[@]}"; do
  # The GitHub API endpoint to retrieve code owners for a specific path:
  # GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/codeowners/errors only lists syntax errors.
  # To resolve owners for a path, parse CODEOWNERS locally.
  echo ""
  echo "Path: $path"

  # Download the CODEOWNERS file (once, but shown here per-path for clarity).
  owners=$(gh api \
    -H "Accept: application/vnd.github+json" \
    "/repos/${REPO}/contents/.github/CODEOWNERS" \
    --jq '.content' | base64 -d 2>/dev/null | \
    # Find the last matching rule for this path (simplified: matches prefix).
    awk -v p="$path" '
      !/^#/ && /^[^#]/ {
        rule = $1
        # Remove leading slash for comparison
        gsub(/^\//, "", rule)
        # Remove trailing glob for prefix check
        gsub(/\*\*$/, "", rule)
        gsub(/\*$/, "", rule)
        if (index(p, rule) == 1 || index(rule, p) == 1) {
          owners = ""
          for (i=2; i<=NF; i++) owners = owners " " $i
          last_owners = owners
        }
      }
      END { print last_owners }
    ' 2>/dev/null || echo "  (could not resolve)")

  if [[ -z "$owners" ]]; then
    echo "  WARNING: No owner found — path may be unowned"
  else
    echo "  Owners:$owners"
  fi
done

echo ""
echo "====================================================="
echo "Audit complete. Review any WARNING entries above."

# List CODEOWNERS syntax errors reported by GitHub:
echo ""
echo "Checking for CODEOWNERS syntax errors..."
gh api "/repos/${REPO}/codeowners/errors" \
  --jq '.errors[] | "  Line " + (.line|tostring) + ": " + .kind + " — " + .suggestion' \
  2>/dev/null || echo "  No syntax errors reported by GitHub."
Rotating Ownership: On-Call Safety Reviewer

Static CODEOWNERS assignments work well when the responsible individuals are available and engaged. In practice, safety review duties can become a bottleneck when a single team or individual owns all safety-sensitive paths. On-call rotation distributes the review burden while maintaining coverage and accountability.
The recommended approach uses GitHub team membership as the rotation mechanism rather than updating CODEOWNERS directly. The CODEOWNERS file points to a team — for example
@myorg/ai-safety-oncall
— and the membership of that team rotates on a weekly or fortnightly schedule. The CODEOWNERS file itself does not change; only the team's member list changes, which GitHub tracks independently.
Create a dedicated on-call team
such as
@myorg/ai-safety-oncall
with a rotating single member. This team is the code owner for high-throughput safety-sensitive paths.
Automate team membership rotation.
A GitHub Actions workflow that runs on a schedule (every Monday morning) updates the oncall team's single member using the GitHub API, driven by a rotation list stored in a YAML file.
Keep the permanent team as a fallback.
For the highest-risk paths, list both
@myorg/ai-safety-oncall
and
@myorg/ai-safety
(the full team). This ensures that if the on-call engineer is unavailable, any member of the full team can still approve.
Track escalation paths.

Document in your internal runbook who to contact if the on-call safety reviewer is unavailable and a time-sensitive PR is blocked. CODEOWNERS enforces the gate but cannot enforce the human response time.
Send PR notification to a shared channel.
Configure GitHub notifications so that any PR requesting review from
@myorg/ai-safety-oncall
is also posted to a team Slack channel. This provides visibility across the team even when the oncall person is the designated reviewer.
Set SLA expectations.

An on-call safety reviewer should have an explicit expectation of maximum response time for code review requests — typically within one business day for routine changes and within four hours for time-sensitive deployments.
Checklist: CODEOWNERS Setup for a New AI Repo
Use this checklist when establishing CODEOWNERS governance in a new generative AI repository or when auditing an existing one.
File and Location
CODEOWNERS file exists at
.github/CODEOWNERS
, repo root, or
docs/CODEOWNERS
The CODEOWNERS file itself (
.github/CODEOWNERS
) has an owner entry — preventing stealth removal of review requirements.
All referenced teams exist in the GitHub organisation and have at least two members.
Branch Protection
Branch protection is enabled on
main
(and any other long-lived branches).
"Require review from Code Owners" is checked in the branch protection rule.
"Dismiss stale pull request approvals when new commits are pushed" is enabled.
"Restrict who can push to matching branches" limits direct pushes to
main
to a small list of admins (or no one).
Asset Coverage
Prompt files and prompt registry have an explicit owner entry (ai-safety team + prompt authors team).
Root system prompt has an additional individual owner (CTO or head of AI) requiring two approvals.
Tool schemas and tool implementations have separate owner entries.
Red-team eval suites are owned exclusively by the AI safety team (not shared with feature engineers).
Eval threshold configuration requires two approvals (eval leads + ai-safety).
Model configuration files have a leadership-level owner in addition to the model ops team.
CI/CD workflow files are owned by the platform engineering team.
Validation and Maintenance
A CI check validates that all required paths have coverage whenever CODEOWNERS changes.
An on-call rotation schedule is documented and team membership rotation is automated.
An annual CODEOWNERS audit is scheduled (or triggered by major team structure changes).
Offboarding checklist includes removing departed engineers from relevant CODEOWNERS teams.
A runbook documents escalation paths when a required reviewer is unavailable.
Key Takeaways

CODEOWNERS maps file patterns to owners and, when branch protection requires code owner review, blocks merges until every matched owner approves — providing mandatory human review at the version-control layer.
Rules are evaluated top-to-bottom and the last matching rule wins. Put general patterns first and specific overrides last to ensure specific rules take effect.

Prompt files, tool definitions, eval configuration, red-team suites, model config, and CI/CD pipelines all warrant explicit owner entries; the CODEOWNERS file itself must be owned to prevent stealth removal.
Red-team safety suites must be owned exclusively by the AI safety team, not shared with feature engineers, to prevent conflicts of interest under deadline pressure.
Use team ownership for routine paths (resilient to individual absence) and add individual ownership only as a supplemental approver for the highest-risk artefacts such as the root system prompt.
A CI validation script that checks required paths have coverage, combined with GitHub API team-existence verification, catches CODEOWNERS errors before they reach main.
On-call rotation using a dedicated GitHub team (whose membership rotates) distributes safety review load without requiring updates to the CODEOWNERS file itself.

Two reviewers are warranted for the root system prompt, any safety instruction change, and any eval threshold reduction — these are the changes with the highest impact and the least visibility in a standard code diff.
